perDomain.verdict: Strong browser-integrated network capture with HAR 1.2 export, request replay with SSRF-safe DNS pinning, HTTP/2 frame construction, and TLS/HTTP fingerprinting. Gaps: no native HTTP/3 support (only fingerprinting), no QUIC probe, no gRPC-specific tools, and HAR exporter hardcodes HTTP/1.1 version strings for all entries.
strengths: HAR 1.2 export with parallel body fetching (concurrency-limited) and optional body inclusion
Request replay with dryRun default, SSRF guard with DNS resolution + IP pinning, redirect following (301/302/303→GET, 307/308→preserve), and authorization policy per request
HTTP/2 probe and raw frame builder (DATA, SETTINGS, PING, WINDOW_UPDATE, RST_STREAM, GOAWAY)
TLS fingerprint computation (GREASE-filtered, JA3-like format) and HTTP fingerprint (method, version, headers, cookies, accept-language)
Bot detection analysis on captured traffic
DNS toolkit: resolve, reverse, probe, CNAME chain, bulk resolve (up to 1000 hosts, 50 concurrency)
Network interception via CDP Fetch domain with glob/regex patterns, batch rules, and stage selection (Request/Response)
Auth extractor scans headers/cookies/URL params/body for Bearer/JWT/session tokens/API keys
Raw HTTP/1.x request builder and plain TCP request sender with authorization policy
Latency stats with percentile computation and RTT measurement (tcp/tls/http probes)
ICMP traceroute and echo probe with configurable TTL/packet size
gaps: HAR exporter always sets httpVersion to 'HTTP/1.1' regardless of actual protocol (HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 traffic misreported)
No native HTTP/3 or QUIC probe tool — only fingerprinting hash computation
No gRPC-specific request builder or protobuf-aware payload inspection
No Wireshark-style packet dissection for captured traffic (relies on protocol-analysis domain for manual packet build)
network_replay_request uses global fetch with manual redirect handling; no HTTP/2 multiplexing or stream priority replay
No certificate transparency (CT) log querying or OCSP stapling inspection
No bandwidth/throttling simulation tools

perDomain.verdict: Functional HTTP/HTTPS interception proxy built on mockttp with CA generation, rule-based forwarding/mock/blocking, and ADB device integration. Limited compared to mitmproxy/Burp Suite: no scriptable request/response transformation, no WebSocket proxying, no upstream proxy chaining, and no traffic decryption introspection beyond mockttp's built-in TLS termination.
strengths: mockttp-based HTTP/HTTPS proxy with auto-generated CA (PKCS#8 normalized for cross-platform compatibility)
CA certificate export for manual trust store installation
Rule-based interception: forward, mock_response (status + body), block (close connection)
ADB device proxy setup: push CA, reverse tcp port, set global http_proxy
Capture buffer with FIFO eviction and URL filtering
gaps: No request/response body inspection or modification in rules (only mock_response with static body)
No scriptable/transform hook support (mitmproxy has Python scripting; Burp has Extender API)
No WebSocket upgrade interception or wss proxying
No upstream proxy chaining (corporate proxy bypass scenarios)
No traffic replay or repeat-from-proxy capabilities
No automatic CA installation on ADB (requires manual Settings install; noted in instructions)
No HAR export from proxy capture (only internal buffer, no HAR serialization)

perDomain.verdict: Solid packet crafting and PCAP I/O with deterministic checksums, but protocol inference is heuristic-based and lacks deep binary protocol analysis. Good for educational/CTF use; industrial reverse engineering would need more structured format grammar support (Kaitai Struct, 010 Editor templates).
strengths: Deterministic Ethernet II, ARP, IPv4/IPv6, ICMP echo packet builders with full header field control (DSCP, ECN, DF/MF, fragment offset, flow label)
Internet checksum application with zero-and-write support, configurable endianness
Classic PCAP read/write (little/big endian, micro/nano precision, configurable link type and snap length); explicitly excludes PCAPNG for determinism
Payload template builder with typed fields (u8/u16/u32/i8/i16/i32/string/bytes), endianness, encoding, padding
Byte-level mutation strategies: set_byte, flip_bit, overwrite_bytes, append_bytes, truncate, increment_integer
Protocol pattern auto-detection from hex samples, field inference, and state machine inference from message sequences
Mermaid state diagram generation from inferred state machines
Protocol fingerprinting against known signatures with field boundary hints
gaps: No TCP/UDP segment builder (only IP packet + ICMP echo; missing transport layer above IP)
No support for protocol grammar definitions (ASN.1, protobuf, msgpack, thrift) beyond simple field pattern
Protocol inference is heuristic/byte-boundary based, not semantic — no true protocol compiler or grammar inference
No integration with Wireshark dissector engine or tshark for automated dissection
No support for reading PCAPNG (modern Wireshark default format)
No support for writing pcapng with enhanced packet blocks, comments, or interface statistics

perDomain.verdict: Competent WebSocket and SSE monitoring via CDP events and in-page EventSource patching. Good frame-level capture with pagination and filtering. Missing: no binary WebSocket payload hex dump, no WebSocket message reconstruction (fragmented frames), and no compression (permessage-deflate) awareness.
strengths: WebSocket monitoring via CDP Network.webSocketFrameSent/Received with RingBuffer-backed frame storage (configurable max 1000-20000 frames)
Frame filtering by direction (sent/received/all), payload regex filter, pagination with offset/limit
Connection tracking with status (connecting/open/closed/error), handshake status, frame counts
SSE monitoring via in-page EventSource wrapper with persistent injection support (evaluateOnNewDocument)
SSE event filtering by sourceUrl and eventType, pagination
Custom event type capture beyond standard message/open/error via addEventListener wrapping
gaps: No binary payload hex decoding for WebSocket frames (payloadSample is raw string, no hex view)
No WebSocket frame fragmentation reassembly (multi-frame messages not reconstructed)
No permessage-deflate compression awareness (compressed payloads appear as binary noise)
No WebSocket handshake header inspection or custom subprotocol negotiation tracking
No Server-Sent Events replay or injection capability (only monitoring)
No streaming bandwidth/rate measurement tools

perDomain.verdict: Impressive TLS tooling with SSLKEYLOGFILE support, active TLS probing, raw socket sessions, and Frida integration for cert pinning bypass. Strong for mobile/browser TLS analysis. Gaps: no TLS 1.3 key derivation (only static keylog parsing), no automated TLS session resumption testing, and no OCSP/CT inspection.
strengths: SSLKEYLOGFILE enable/parse/disable with secret lookup by client_random, summary by label distribution
TLS payload decryption with configurable algorithm (default aes-256-gcm) and auth tag
Active TLS endpoint probing with full handshake metadata: cipher, ALPN, certificate chain, hostname validation, session reuse, timing
Certificate pinning bypass strategy generation per platform (android/ios/desktop) with instructions
Frida-based cert pinning bypass delegating to binary-instrument domain (BoringSSL, Chrome, OkHttp support)
Raw TLS handshake parsing from hex with ClientHello SNI/cipher suite/extension extraction
Certificate chain parsing from hex with SHA-256 fingerprints
Stateful TCP and TLS sessions with read-until-delimiter, write, close (graceful or force)
Raw TCP/UDP send/listen for one-shot or timeout-bound interactions
WebSocket client session management (open/send/read/close) with TLS options
SSRF validation on all network targets, custom CA bundle support, allowInvalidCertificates and skipHostnameCheck policies
gaps: TLS keylog parsing is static file-based; no live capture from running process without SSLKEYLOGFILE env (Frida path exists but separate)
No TLS 1.3 specific key schedule or early data (0-RTT) inspection tools
No OCSP stapling status inspection or certificate transparency log verification
No automated TLS session resumption / ticket rotation testing
No HTTP/2 ALPN-aware WebSocket upgrade (only raw ws/wss)
Raw socket tools are one-shot or single-connection; no persistent server mode for multi-client testing
No TLS middlebox or DPI evasion fingerprint randomization

perDomain.verdict: Well-rounded GraphQL toolkit with introspection, field enumeration via suggestion errors, query extraction from captured traffic, and replay. Good SSRF protection and browser/Node dual-path execution. Gaps: no batch query analysis, no query complexity/cost analysis, no subscription testing, and no persisted query inspection.
strengths: GraphQL introspection with standard query, browser or Node fetch, schema preview with truncation safety
Field enumeration via server suggestion errors (Did you mean ...) with introspection fallback, configurable depth (1-6)
Query extraction from captured network traces (fetch/XHR/in-page monitors) with deduplication by URL+operationName+query+variables
GraphQL replay with variable injection, operationName, custom headers, browser/Node dual path
SSRF protection via validateExternalEndpoint (blocks private/reserved addresses) and validateBrowserEndpoint (same-origin policy check)
Persistent script replacement via CDP request interception for modifying GraphQL client scripts
Call graph analysis for runtime function tracing in page context
gaps: No GraphQL batch query analysis or alias-based attack detection
No query complexity/cost analysis or depth-limit testing
No subscription (WebSocket-based GraphQL) testing or replay
No persisted query (APQ) hash inspection or automatic persisted query probing
No GraphQL directive-based introspection bypass detection
No federation/schema stitching-specific tools (gateway introspection, entity resolution)
graphql_enum_schema concurrency parameter is reserved/future expansion (not implemented)

criticalFindings.finding: HAR exporter hardcodes HTTP version to 'HTTP/1.1' for all entries regardless of actual protocol (HTTP/2 or HTTP/3). This corrupts HAR fidelity and misleads downstream analysis in Chrome DevTools, Wireshark, or performance tools.
criticalFindings.recommendation: Track actual protocol version from CDP Network.responseReceived/loadingFinished events and emit correct httpVersion ('HTTP/2', 'HTTP/3', 'HTTP/1.1') in HAR entries.
criticalFindings.industryBenchmark: Chrome DevTools HAR export preserves accurate protocol version; Wireshark dissects HTTP/2 streams correctly.

criticalFindings.finding: Replay engine uses global fetch with manual redirect handling and no HTTP/2 multiplexing support. For HTTP/2 origins, replay may downgrade to HTTP/1.1 or fail on connection coalescing, breaking fidelity for modern APIs.
criticalFindings.recommendation: Integrate Node.js http2 module for HTTP/2-aware replay with stream priority and header compression preservation.

warningFindings.finding: TLS fingerprint computation implements a custom format (not standard JA3/JA3S/JA4). While internally consistent, interoperability with existing bot-detection databases (FingerprintJS, Cloudflare JA3 blocklists) requires manual mapping.
warningFindings.recommendation: Add optional ja3 and ja3s output fields alongside the custom format for industry compatibility.

warningFindings.finding: Rule actions limited to forward/mock_response/block. No request/response body transformation, no regex replacement, no script hook — significantly less capable than mitmproxy's Python scripting or Burp's Extender API.
warningFindings.recommendation: Add a 'transform' action type accepting a JavaScript/QuickJS script for request/response mutation, or integrate with sandbox-sdk for safe script execution.

warningFindings.finding: Explicitly excludes PCAPNG support. Modern Wireshark and tcpdump default to pcapng; users must convert files before analysis.
warningFindings.recommendation: Add pcapng read support (at least for Enhanced Packet Blocks with common link types) to eliminate friction with modern capture tools.

warningFindings.finding: No TCP or UDP segment builder — transport layer above IP is missing. Users cannot craft complete TCP SYN packets or UDP datagrams with checksums for full stack testing.
warningFindings.recommendation: Add tcp_segment_build and udp_datagram_build with sequence/ack numbers, flags, window size, and UDP checksum.

warningFindings.finding: Binary WebSocket payloads are not hex-decoded; payloadSample stores raw string which may be corrupted for non-UTF8 binary data. No fragmentation reassembly.
warningFindings.recommendation: Add hex payload output for binary frames and implement multi-frame message reassembly using FIN and opcode continuity rules.

warningFindings.finding: No OCSP stapling status or certificate transparency (CT) log inspection in TLS probe results. Modern security assessment requires CT visibility (SCT list parsing).
warningFindings.recommendation: Parse and report OCSP staple status, SCT list count, and CT log names from TLS handshake extensions.

warningFindings.finding: Concurrency parameter is documented but not implemented (reserved for future expansion). Users may expect parallel enumeration speedup.
warningFindings.recommendation: Implement bounded concurrent field probing with Promise.allSettled and configurable concurrency, or remove the parameter from the schema until implemented.

infoFindings.finding: No HTTP/3 or QUIC probe tool exists. Only fingerprinting hash computation is available. For full HTTP/3 analysis, users must rely on external tools (curl --http3, nghttp3).
No gRPC-specific request builder or protobuf-aware payload inspection. gRPC-Web traffic can be captured but not decoded without manual protobuf schema.
Proxy capture buffer is in-memory only with no persistence or HAR serialization. For long-running captures, buffer eviction causes data loss.
TLS session resumption and 0-RTT early data testing are not supported. No ticket rotation or PSK binder inspection tools.
No GraphQL subscription (WebSocket-based) testing tools. No automatic persisted query (APQ) hash probing.

ourGaps: No live capture without env var
No direct Wireshark integration (manual file export needed)

overallVerdict: The Network & Security domain group is functionally comprehensive for browser-integrated network analysis, TLS inspection, and GraphQL API testing. It exceeds typical MCP tool suites in depth (HAR export, SSRF-safe replay, TLS keylog, packet crafting) but falls short of industrial standalone tools (mitmproxy, Burp, Wireshark) in areas requiring deep protocol dissection, scriptable interception, HTTP/3/QUIC support, and live kernel-level capture. The critical gaps are HAR version accuracy and HTTP/2 replay fidelity. With targeted fixes, this group can serve as a credible lightweight alternative for automated security pipelines.
